skill.name: mcp-apps-builder
skill.description: Guides developers through building MCP servers and apps with mcp-use, including tools, resources, prompts, widgets, authentication, deployment, and response patterns.
skill.summary: Build production-ready MCP servers and interactive MCP apps with mcp-use best practices.
skill.author: mcp-use

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows the skill is a Markdown documentation pack for mcp-use development rather than executable code. Most alerts are false positives from code fences, navigation links, license text, and intentionally shown examples; the remaining risk is that the guidance may lead agents to run shell commands, deploy services, access environment variables, call external APIs, or persist browser state in legitimate MCP projects.

medium_findings.title: Legitimate Shell and Deployment Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for operational risk, not malicious behavior. The skill tells agents to scaffold and run mcp-use projects, and deployment guidance includes commands that can publish services or pass secrets as environment variables. This is expected for a developer skill, but users should review commands before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and semantically relevant to the skill. They are not hidden or malicious, but they can change the local project or deployment state.

medium_findings.title: Examples Use External APIs and Environment Secrets
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for sensitive-context guidance, with benign intent. The documentation shows API keys read from environment variables and fetch calls to third-party services. This is normal MCP server instruction, but it increases risk if copied without secret management or domain review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network and environment access patterns are real in the examples. The surrounding text frames them as implementation guidance, not exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Markdown Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for most external command, weak crypto, filesystem, and obfuscation alerts. The flagged backticks, links, HS256 references, and ../ navigation entries appear inside Markdown examples, headings, or license text, not executable package code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative sampled locations show documentation and code fences rather than executable files. No evidence found that these snippets run automatically when the skill is installed.

low_findings.title: Browser Local Storage Example
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for browser storage access in an example, with low severity. The advanced widget guide demonstrates localStorage for UI preferences, which can be safe for non-sensitive state but should not be used for tokens or secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The localStorage calls are directly present in a widget example. The code stores favorites, not credentials, so the risk is limited to misuse by downstream implementers.

low_findings.title: Overbroad Skill Activation Language
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW as marketplace content. The skill uses mandatory language for all MCP server work and instructs agents to read reference files before implementation. This is not a prompt injection attempt because it does not ask to bypass security review or override system instructions, but it is broad operational guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text is strong but scoped to MCP development. No evidence found of commands to ignore marketplace audit instructions, conceal behavior, or claim pre-approval.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Execution Examples in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes shell command examples such as project scaffolding, local development, authentication checks, and deployment. They are user-visible examples, not hidden execution paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Commands are clearly documented and expected for the use case. Risk depends on whether an agent executes them without user review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network and Credential Handling Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples show fetch calls, OAuth endpoints, API keys, database URLs, and environment files. These are necessary for MCP server guidance, but they require careful handling in generated projects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly reference secrets and network endpoints. The context is instructional and includes warnings against committing secrets.

content.user_title: Build MCP Apps with mcp-use Patterns
content.value_statement: Building MCP servers requires many decisions about tools, resources, prompts, widgets, authentication, and deployment. This skill provides structured mcp-use guidance so Claude, Codex, and Claude Code can follow consistent implementation patterns.
actual_capabilities: Routes agents to focused references for mcp-use tools, resources, prompts, widgets, authentication, deployment, and response helpers.
Provides TypeScript patterns for defining MCP tools with Zod schemas and response helpers.
Explains how to build React widgets that connect to MCP tool outputs and client state.
Documents OAuth provider patterns for WorkOS, Supabase, and custom identity providers.
Covers deployment steps, environment variable handling, and production readiness checks.
Lists common mistakes and security checks for MCP server development.
limitations: It is documentation and guidance, not a runnable MCP server template by itself.
Examples may need updates for the installed mcp-use version and project structure.
Authentication and deployment examples require real provider credentials and configuration.
Generated commands and environment changes should be reviewed before execution.

use_cases.title: Create a New MCP Server
use_cases.description: Use the quickstart and architecture guidance to scaffold a server and add the first tool or resource.

use_cases.title: Add Interactive MCP Widgets
use_cases.description: Use the widget references to build React interfaces that render tool results and manage client-side state.

use_cases.title: Prepare MCP Apps for Production
use_cases.description: Use authentication, deployment, environment variable, and security checklist guidance before publishing a server.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Server
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-apps-builder skill to create a basic mcp-use server with one validated tool. Explain each file you add.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Resource
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-apps-builder references to add a read-only MCP resource for project configuration data. Include validation and response helpers.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Widget Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the widget guidance to add an MCP tool that returns a React widget for browsing and selecting records.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-apps-builder security and deployment guidance to review this MCP server for auth, secret handling, errors, rate limits, and deployment gaps.

output_examples.input: Create a weather MCP server with a simple tool and secure API key handling.
output: A server scaffold plan that uses mcp-use conventions.
A validated weather tool with clear input descriptions.
Environment variable guidance and a reminder not to commit secrets.
Manual verification steps for local testing.

output_examples.input: Add a searchable widget to an existing MCP app.
output: A tool and widget design that separates backend data from UI state.
React widget behavior for loading, selection, empty states, and errors.
Notes about styling, auto sizing, and accessibility expectations.

output_examples.input: Review my MCP server before deployment.
output: A checklist covering schema descriptions, input validation, secrets, error handling, and rate limits.
Deployment steps that verify authentication and environment variables.
Concrete issues to fix before publishing.

best_practices: Read the specific reference file for the MCP primitive before editing a server.
Use Zod schemas, response helpers, and explicit error handling for every tool.
Keep secrets in environment variables and review deployment commands before running them.
anti_patterns: Scaffolding a new MCP project inside an unrelated existing project without user approval.
Returning raw objects or thrown errors instead of the documented response helpers.
Copying example API keys, URLs, or storage patterns without adapting them to the real security model.

faq.question: Is this skill a complete MCP server?
faq.answer: No. It is a guide and reference set for building MCP servers and apps with mcp-use.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include widget guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes references for widget basics, state, interactivity, advanced patterns, and UI guidelines.

faq.question: Does it cover authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers WorkOS, Supabase, and custom OAuth provider patterns.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The files are documentation, but they guide shell commands, deployment, network calls, and secret handling.

faq.question: What should users review before running generated work?
faq.answer: Users should review shell commands, deployment targets, environment variables, API domains, and any storage of user data.
